text: The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar to grasp the descending sigmoid junction, tenting it up in the anterior aspect of the abdominal cavity. The Tip-up grasper holds the anticipated proximal division point while the vessel sealer is used to burn the mesentery, aiming towards the third arm. The IMA pedicle is tented to the right lower quadrant, and the IMV is visible running parallel to the IMA. The surgeon continues to burn up the mesentery, referencing the third arm as a fixed point to avoid deviation, until reaching the mesenteric border of the descending sigmoid junction. Final epipelvic appendages are dissected off in preparation for an anastomosis.

The surgeon cleans the surgical area before amputating tissue in preparation for an anvil insertion. The assistant pulls the divided inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle to the right lower quadrant, while the surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar to pull the descending mesocolon to the left. The vessel sealer is positioned between these structures, aiming north towards the third arm for precise tissue division.

The surgeon administers 2.5 cc of ICG followed by a 10 cc flush of normal saline, observing the tissue perfusion within thirty seconds. The descending sigmoid junction is shown with the epiploic appendages being divided, and the tissue appears perfectly perfused after devascularization.

The surgeon checks the mesenteric orientation, focusing on the divided IMV and IMA pedicles. The camera view is adjusted to trace the cut edge of the mesentery, which is slightly to the right. The assistant pushes in the staple to ensure proper alignment for the end-to-end anastomosis setup.
